commentBody: I find myself unduly tickled that both TERRI GROSS and ARI SHAPIRO fit 56A.

Can someone explain to me how to contact the NYTimes Games staff to correct my statistics?  I do like to keep those for personal inspiration, but inexplicably it has one of my stats completely incorrect.  Any humans one can speak to?
That was one of the hardest recent NYT Friday puzzles I can recall. A very satisfying workout!
This is exactly what I want from a Friday. Started out breezy but got trickier later on; I finished in 13:42.
Oops, thanks guys

"Want to join my trigonometry class?"
"Oooh, where do I sine?"

(And pardon the tangent, but if you love the course, you might get your degree in it.)

I had penciled in TollS for 28D, so coSYREADS seemed obvious for 30A, but then I couldn't figure out c__KOSHER. (Eat and keep are the only relevant verbs I can think of.)  Or ZENSTA_lS or why 35A could be "ale", for that matter. 

It's funny how having text filled in makes it hard to see the alternatives. Once I took out toll (left the s though) and o, and put in EAT, I was better able to think of what else 28D might be. 

You could say it was a taxing process...
On the west coast, we call them “California stops”

Finally got it when I realized it wasn’t HOLYMOley

L

Great puzzle today, lots of fun clues.  Maybe on the easy side for a Friday whereas yesterday was hard for a Thursday.

SAPIOSEXUAL might be one of my favorite crossword answers in a while.

When I had HOLYMO--- of course I filled it in HOLYMOLEY at first.  Which made that area tougher for me.  Trying to figure out what could fit in ZEN_TA__L when I also thought the laundry one might be LINTFILTERS.

TERRYGROSS crossed with TARA also mad that area tough as I knew neither of those names.

37A brings to mind the old joke about the Buddhist monk who walks up to a hot dog vendor and says "Make me one with everything."

After a brief chuckle at the monk's joke, the vendor hands him his hot dog with everything and says "That'll be $4 please."  The monk hands over a $10 bill and waits while the vendor just stares back at him.  Awkwardly the monk asks "What about my change?"

"Oh," replies the hot dog vendor, "Change must come from within".

Sometimes I play around with swapping which entries could go with which clues:

Breezy things enjoyed at beach: ZEN STATES
Attracted to intellect over looks: WHERE DO I SIGN
We have a mutual friend: HOLY MOSES

But the do-it-yourself tornado can only ever always be ACME!

Where is the line on duplication between clues and answers? 'Kashrut' and 'KOSHER' have the same root, so I thought that kind of went against the traditional rules of crosswords. Why not, "Adhere to Jewish dietary rules"? Then there's not even a hint of duplication.

Here’s what I love about Friday puzzles. I start out thinking I know nothing. I get a few gimmes then I surprise myself with how much I can deduce. Almost done but two or three that absolutely stump me (ah sapiosexual). Darn I have to google. But I finish, and it’s sort of thrilling. I’m getting better at this. 

Saturday comes around and I’m cooked. Thank goodness for Sunday through Thursday.
Thank you, thank you, thank you! Beautiful Friday puzzle like the old days. Yummy. The entire right half gave me fits.

My PAPA tells a great story about having lunch with Orson Welles in Barcelona in the late ‘60s. Dad was living a bohemian existence in his twenties, lillypadding around Europe and developing his painting craft while studying the works of the great masters. He fell in with a sort of artist collective there—writers, musicians, painters, et al—and they would pal around the museums and cafes during the day and the galleries and clubs by night, often getting invited to swank society parties that were looking to add a little color and flair to their guest lists. One night they ended up crashing a late night afterparty in some private residence on beach and there was Mr. Welles, amidst a flock of other celebrities and local well-to-dos. According to my father he was “feeling no pain” and was rushing out the door with a young woman on his arm, but he stopped when he saw them and asked through a slur, “are you beautiful people artists by chance?” They chatted for moment and Welles invited them to lunch the next day at his favorite restaurant, 7 Portes. “The paella is historic. Meet me under the colonnade, not before 2. Never before 2!” They arrived the next afternoon to find Mr. Welles seated at his personal table, holding court and regaling an enrapt audience with ripping yarns from Hollywood and parts abroad. Welles had apparently been there for hours, as the empty plates of at least three separate meals could attest, impossibly drunk, and wearing nothing but a fruit hat.

I'm doing an old archive puzzle and the clues are stuff like "Decorticates", "German Expressionist Emil", "Savoyard guard", and other words/things no one has ever heard of that you have to actually puzzle out. It's challenging and interesting. This is not. Really sick of every weekday essentially being a Monday/Tuesday

I’ll give a fuller review tomorrow, I’m just too tired; but just gotta say if SAPIOSEXUALS is really a thing, I wish some of them were around when I went to college, cause I sure never met any! And now I’m taken. Way to miss the revolution.

That was quite the back and forth struggled between the puzzle and me.

It no-hit me through the first half of the game, then I managed to get a scratch single, and second base on a throwing error, yada, yada and I finally scored.  Now get some crosses and I'm only my way.

But then it sacked me  well be hind the line of scrimmage, then allowing no yardage at all against the stiff defense of clues.  

Then I saw an open player downfield, made a spectacular pass and they put it right in the back of the net.

I'm thinking the fight is over when it lands a crushing right hook, a 3x3 part of the puzzle that I simply cannot get.  Barely managed to finish the round on my legs.

Then I caught all of a second shot, which landed in a perfect spot on the green, for an eagle three, Victory!

The puzzle points out the two squares I'd forgotten, dashing my ALLTOOSOON celebration.  Then the puzzles drains back to back treys and suddenly those two squares had turned into six squares, after retreating on RAIL and RODDY.

I don't remember much of the run after that.  Everything goes kind of dreamlike in the last part of  marathon.  I was ahead, then it pulled ahead, and I came back, and somehow we'd both collapsed across the finish line.

Photo finish.  Me, by a nose.  A $2.00 bet paid $7.25.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
Chewy Wednesday.

Thanks, New York Times!

"Classic Nabisco brand (no, the other one)"

Hah, that's a classic one for the regular NYT Crossword solvers. Wish you had kept that one.

Another fun Friday!

But please tell me I'm not the only one who cringes at the sight of that hyphen in fund-raising. It's like nails on a chalk-board for me, Every Single Time. Come on NYT please update your style guide, please?!? 🙏🏼
W(asn't)T(hat)F(un)!

Done, but I wouldn't say all too soon.  Some easy fills with catchy clues, and some I'd never heard of.  

Well, sociosexual—I always dreamed that would be my signature label, but everything was always so muddled up I could never tell.  As they say, beauty is often in the eye of the beholder, and people get a lot better looking when they make you feel smart.

Thanks for the Friday, Heman Mehta. You craft a great looking puzzle.
All the constructors should add their fave cut clues! Loved reading those
Some good cluing alongside slightly too much terrible trivia.  Not a breeze for those outside of the US specific sport/media bubble

My initial answer for 37A was “DALAILAMA” from the Karl Stefaovic joke he made to Dalai Lama making him one with everything(the joke being it was at a hot dog cart.) Frustratingly enough — intentional or unintentional — it fit, which was obviously incorrect but still a bit of fun
Holy moley! I got stuck in the middle for a while, wondering “when do I get it”? But then Moses showed up and showed me where to sign.

Very clever clues. Seemed like there were multiple layers of misdirection. Rather difficult but all the more satisfying to complete. Key was to give up on some answers I was very confident of. Honestly thought I wouldn’t get it.

Don’t know about a “Lansing Left,” but when we moved to the Detroit area, I found out about the “Michigan Left,” wherein making a left turn at most major street intersections requires proceeding through the intersection as if going straight, making a U-turn using the lane specifically designed for that purpose a couple of hundred feet past the intersection, and turning right at the intersection when you get back to it. Seemed unnecessarily complicated at first, but it made sense for traffic flow once I got the hang of it.

There is a Michigan left, but it is very specific to boulevards. Instead of going left at a light, you go right, cross all of the lanes to get in a left-turn lane that is between intersections, and then go left. It's supposedly better for traffic flow, but it takes a whole lotta getting used to if you are not from here!

Have I been watching the Stanley Cup playoffs? No. Have I ever watched an entire game of hockey? No. (I'm allowed to live in Michigan only with a special indulgence.) 
If you had asked me what icing is in hockey, without telling me it's a penalty, I would have assumed it meant putting the puck in play, putting it on the ice.
Great Friday puzzle. Thanks. Loved 'What'd I miss' especially being on top of Terry Gross who's NPR show I miss. Enjoyed the smaller clues like alarm for dream interrupter.
SAPIOSEXUAL held me up at the end, but the rest of the puzzle solved pretty quickly once I got a foothold.  Took 43% less time than average.

Never considered that ELMO was a monster. 
The clue for ROWDY [neither demure nor mindful] was nagging at me. Finally got it this morning. It's a play on a Tik Tok poster's phrase,  Very demure, very mindful, that went viral in 2024. 

Solved this on the quicker side of Friday time, but no complaints today.
Any crossword with a hockey related clue is an automatic 5 star for me!

Out here we have always called ROLLINGSTOPS California rolls (no, not the sushi!), but rolling stops makes more sense.   I had also put HOLYMOlEy initially, but corrected it to HOLYMOSES after the botched EAlYREADS.  Also got a kick out of 31D- They're filled with bacon? leading to ATMS!

10D [Someone attracted to intellect over looks, say] SAPIOSEXUAL

Thanks to the column for explaining that "homo sapiens" refers to a "thinking" brain. I had never "thought" of it.

Of course, there are many other words with this root.
"To think" is "savoir" in French and "sapere" in Italian. There's also "savoir faire" and "savant" and "quien sabe" and "je ne sais quoi," to name a few.

Not quite the same, but along the same line: "Je pense donc je suis" or "Cogito ergo sum."

When I see Hemant’s name atop a puzzle, I spontaneously break into a smile.  I love his creations, and let me give you one reason why -- spark.

His puzzles are alive, filled with it, that is, words we’ve never seen before in the Times grid, and their never-seen-before clues.  When I say filled, I mean significantly.  I did the calculation today – 58% of the white squares in the puzzle belong to debut answers. What!?!?!?!?  Think about that!

Now, debut answers aren’t automatically sparky, i.e., here are some potential debuts, never having been in the Times puzzle before: ADVERTISED, BACK MUSCLE, and AUTODIALER.  Snore.

But today’s debuts – do have buzz, IMO, all eleven of them, including WHAT’D I MISS, WHERE DO I SIGN, ALL TOO SOON, ROLLING STOPS, and TERRY GROSS.  Such buzz is the rule in Hemant’s puzzles.  

Without a doubt, Hemant, I’ll once again light up with a smile when I see your name.  You are a Crosslandia gift.  Thank you!
My first shot at 30D was EAT NO PORK. Too bad I was too clever by half!
Disappointed when I realized AMYLS couldn't work at 40 across.
This one came together beautifully for me. Bit of a rough start and then everything just fell into place. Well done!
Breezy Friday with no groan-worthy fill. Great puzzle, only wish some of the cluing were a little trickier so I could have chewed on it longer!

Found this one challenging, and never felt I was tuned to right frequency today. Solved it without help, but took over half-an-hour. Didn't know TERRYGROSS or TARA VanDerveer, and have never seen SAPIOSEXUAL before (though it was simple enough to figure out from the very direct clue).

Made quite the mess by entering STENO rather than TONES for "Apt anagram of NOTES", figuring that, of course, a stenographer takes notes. 

Looking back at my filled grid, it seems that the entries themselves were not the issue. For whatever reason, the clues simply weren't clicking with me today.

Personal peeve--having linked clues for *consecutive* horizontal entries that appear in the wrong order, i.e., don't use that style of cluing when you have HOP LINDY sitting in the grid, rather than LINDY HOP. 

All in all, a perfectly fine Friday puzzle that I found surprisingly difficult to solve.

I haven't started the puzzle yet...but in the spirit of "It's too easy," I wanted to point out that The Bee has ....allowed an S to be among the  chosen letters!

Repent!  The End is Nigh!
Anything with Terry Gross in it is stellar.

Perfectly fine Friday puzzle.  It seemed a tad on the easy side, but that happens with such regularity that we're past complaining.
And YMMV.

I liked seeing TERRY GROSS, but that was kinda easy for us Public Radio types.  None of the longer clues were especially tricky, with the exception of SAPIOSEXUAL, which was new to me.  But, if they weren't tricky, at least they felt fresh.

Having included "Bacon" in the clue for 31-Down (for ATM), it felt like a seriously missed opportunity not to include "Bacon" in the clue for 30-Down (for EAT KOSHER).  It might have got past the editors' complaints about "Pig out!"

So, to be honest I’m of three minds about this puzzle. I’m not going to say “too easy” because there were a few spots that gave me some pause especially the SE, and there were a reasonable share of clever clues and interesting fill. My second mind was the number of clunky clues that I don’t expect in a Mehta puzzle—the kashrut one (which is not his fault), the ROWDY one, and ROLLINGSTOPS. The last isn’t really a maneuver, in that it’s not something deliberate you do for a definite reason—or if it is then that contradicts the lazy part. I know it doesn’t have to be perfect, it just rubbed me the wrong way. Then finally there were just the usual Friday gimmes like USSR and others that just feel like they are phoned in. Two other examples—maybe STENO could have been an alternate for TONES but still I feel like a basic anagram clue like has no place in a Friday or Saturday. And ZION. Why do we need the hint that it’s the last alphabetically? Here’s the thing, I was already considering RAZING for the cross, although there were likely others. Then I need to check the downs. If there were some other trivia about ZION that I knew, great, then I feel a sense of satisfaction. Or maybe some other crosses that make sense. But when I see “last alphabetically” I’m done. I don’t have to know ZION yet or anything else, I immediately know that RAZING is right with no extra work on my part. It’s just why I’d like every Friday and Saturday clue to have an extra kick in it.

Given the (amusing) use of "Bacon" in 31-Down, it seems like a seriously missed opportunity that "Bacon" wasn't used in the clue for 30-Down.

It might have been even better than our constructor's (amusing) "Pig out!"

There's a joke I first heard told by a dyed-in-the-wool southerner (excuse the excessive hyphenation) regarding a rolling stop.
(Feel free to embellish as needed for your nekka the woods).

The Po leese pulls over a driver for rolling through a stop sign. An argument ensues over the difference between a complete stop and rolling stop. The driver expresses his failure to grasp the difference between the two.
Po leese says, "Allow me to demonstrate." He pulls the river out of the vehicle and commences to whuppin' the tar out the guy and says, "Now should I do a rolling stop, or a complete stop?"

Well... tough one for me and had to look some things up. Ten debut answers and a couple of them completely unfamiliar terms. No big deal - that's just me. And... puzzle find today. A Sunday from November 14, 1993 by Norma Steinberg with the title "Where in the World?" I'll just list some sample theme answers: VENICEDINNER LAOSRESORT SEVILLESERVANT PUSANBOOTS ELBAGREASE CHILERECEPTION BEIJINGSUITS Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=11/14/1993&h=26a See you tomorrow. ....

Well this was probably easy if you knew all the stuff I didn’t know and all the sayings I never heard of. I gave up half way through because it had stopped being fun. I revealed just so I could see all the answers I didn’t know. I’m wondering if aside from living under a rock I’m aging out of these puzzles.

The Pittsburgh left is when the driver going straight signals to the driver across to make his/her left turn. It isn’t jumping in front of the oncoming vehicle to make the left turn… it’s being politely allowed to make the left turn. Pittsburgh drivers are nice that way.  Philadelphia, I don’t know.

For the many fans of TERRY GROSS (and Public Radio afficionados, in general), you must see this short film by Mike Birbiglia (made for Ira Glass's "This American Life") which plays on TERRY's public persona to tremendous effect! https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1LFl_Ey2BkY
May 2026 has 2 PRs for me - my worst Friday and now my best Friday ever! This was a fun puzzle for me
I loved the puzzle, but I loved the column even more :)
Thank you, Sean, for sharing your experience with Hades 2. I empathize.

Fun puzzle! Thank you, Hemant! (And thank you for your Instagram post about the process - @friendly.atheist) Will listen to some Sara Bareilles music while eating one of my toddler’s cracker snacks. I’m sure most of us put the other one first. Holy Moses, let us live in peace.
My mother told me playing video games will get me nowhere, but I had the last laugh with 12D
What do "notes" have  in common with "tones"? This was really really disappointing...

Raise your hand if you didn't know that the clue for ROWDY [Neither demure nor mindful] was an allusion to an internet meme created by Jools Lebron in 2024. [Insert emoji of raised hand.] https://www.youtube.com/shorts/gFB2-wFJv5c Here's a video that discusses how the meme went viral: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tL4WZlRJMZA

The SAPIOSEXUAL ROLLINGSTOP is when you’re driving by the library, and *The One* comes out the doors, with those horn-rimmed glasses peeking over their stack of books, and you are smitten, but they just roll their eyes, so you move on.
At least here in NB, it is.

(The puzzle also had me slow down a few times, but I liked it.)

PALME D'OR didn't fit, but I knew 4D...lost ALL TOO SOON to a world that needed him.

An early misdirection:  I tried to fit Nina TOTENBERG in there; TSK, 53D!  And me a long-time listener to NPR!  I loved WGUC in Cincinnati and WKSU in NE Ohio.  Mississippi Public Radio has managed to schedule "All Things Considered"--but only from 6 to 8 P.M.! Instead of mid-afternoon as God intended.  32D and 57A !(Well, almost.)

I've never heard of 10D; (I expect I have a lot of company)...but I get it.  An example would be, say, characters played by Josh Malina.  ( Don't most folks seek features that are going to last?)

TIL - SAPIOSEXUAL. 
Is there a term for people attracted to humor over looks? Asking for a friend! 🤣

I'm from the generation that actually did the LINDY HOP; I knew that CASH is king; and I checked to see that 37A was a plural of some sort before writing in HOLY MOSES with confidence.  QWING to that, the NE was a slam dunk for me.  Would that the rest of the puzzle would have presented such EASY READS.

SAPIOSEXUAL was a huge WT (you-know-what) for me (love the clue for the latter).  Is "agitate" STIR or ROIL -- I have only the "I".  If it's STIR, then "avoid rage-quitting" is SIT BY.  If it's ROIL, then the answer is...RETRY???!!!  WT (you-know-what)???  This to me is one of the most oddball clue/answers of all time.  

I struggled to prevail, but prevail I did.  It felt like it took forever.  And what I know about myself is that, even with three times the knowledge of arcane trivia that I possess right now, I will never be a good time solver because of my tendency to obsess over things I can't figure out rather than to simply LINDY HOP blithely around the grid, filling in all the things situated somewhere else that I DO know and would have filled in long ago had I gotten to them in a respectable amount of time.  Puzzle tournaments that care about such a thing as solving time: Don't hold your breath for my appearance anytime soon.

userLocation: in the garden in the wilderness
commentBody:
oh i like a chewy puzzle such as today's!

first, a little workout for my brain, then off to the gym for the rest of me

happy friday, everyone!

I enjoyed the breezy fill and like Hemant's puzzles in general, but c'mon this was a Wednesday at best. Some of us have Friday work we're trying to avoid and need the challenge of a Friday puzzle to use up time!

Imo there's a pretty strong distinction between SASS and guff. The first carries a connotation of rebellious defiance while the second is more obfuscatory. But maybe that's just my age showing, as I know in modern parlance SASS is viewed as a (mostly) positive refusal to conform (although again I'm a boomer, so maybe I'm missing some subtleties.)
"They might make you one with everything" may be my all-time favourite clue.
Lots of misdirection to slow me down. Set, and achieved, a personal goal to solve top to bottom with no skipping ahead and no backtracking. If a puzzle looks “too easy,” add a challenge of your own.

Today's poem made from words found in today's puzzle
a/ can you catch a feeling unravel the edges catch it let it go catch it ask yourself in the brew of the feeling or in the explosive rubble or perhaps you can d/ come to attention in a dream in the thwack of birth look inward be mindful? a/ what’d i miss?
Nice pic of Durham with snow.
Was not a particularly quick one for me, but came together once I let go of "steno" and "RagS on". When done, had to go look up SAPIO to discover its root.
Michigan left, its used in Michigan. Indicating you are making a left turn in order to complete a right turn... confusing unless you're in the middle of it.
Took a bit to find the toehold but flowed smoothly after that.  Also finished up yesterday's.  Both were good.  Many thanks.
According to ngram viewer, SAPIOSEXUAL was barely used until around 2008 when it saw a steep rise. I suspect that had to do with the concurrent rise in the use of dating apps.

And... my usual late puzzle find. A Sunday from July 30, 1961 by Norma Schier with the title: "Playthings." Some theme answers: ONCEINALIFETIME TWOFORTHESEESAW THETHREEPENNYOPERA FIVEFINGEREXERCISE THESEVENYEARITCH TENLITTLEINDIANS ELEVENSES Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=7/30/1961&h=16d ....
I've read a lot of the comments and don't see an explanation for ZEN STATES, can someone explain it?

@Amy

BANANAPPEAL

CHUCKLEF***

ps

as for that lazy braking thing
esp at a STOP SIGN
in the land of SUSHI & SUNSHINE
have heard some refer to it as 

A CALIFORNIA ROLL
Anagram clues on a Friday? :(

I was once an election campaign volunteer in Philadelphia working on a phone bank when the next name on my list was a person named T. Gross. A woman with a pleasant voice answered the phone and after I made my spiel, she told me that she was a great supporter of my candidate. As she spoke, her voice sounded more and more familiar. Then I looked back at by list, and saw T. Gross? And asked if she were THE Terry Gross?" She confessed it was her, and over the next few minutes we had a delightful conversation, which was mostly her asking me questions about the election.
This is exactly what I want in a Wordplay column.  Well done, Sean; more, please!

>>Tone: a musical "event," as it were, consisting of: 1) pitch 2) duration--how long does it last? 3) dynamics--i.e. how loud is it? 3) timbre--does it sound like a flute or a violin? >>Pitch: sonic vibrations at particular frequency--for instance, 440Hz=A in the octave above middle C. Technically, only within that octave--to speak of all the A's, octaves above and below, one should use "pitch class." >>Note: the graphical representation, on paper, usually, of a tone. Does anyone--even among musicians--make these distinctions, in real-world conversation? Heck no! Too busy trying to stay in tune! OK go: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=12zJw9varYE&list=RD12zJw9varYE&start_radio=1
Bagel baker, might make you one with everything.

One last puzzle find. Dad jokes - a puzzle from Friday December 12, 1975 by Dennis Listort. Three 15 letter grid-spanning theme answers: WHATDIDDELAWARE WHENDIDMARYLAND IDONTKNOWALASKA Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=12/12/1975&h=17a ...

In CA “we” (not necessarily everyone) call a rolling stop a California Roll. I see it every time I’m out and it’s particularly dangerous for us scooter/moped/motorcycle riders. I was almost hit by a guy in a truck just yesterday because he didn’t stop completely when it was my turn to go. 
In MN there’s a thing called a “Minnesota Wedge,” where you’re stuck behind two slow-moving vehicles traveling next to each other. No clue if that’s universally known or if it’s just something my husband came up with, but I have witnessed it many times.
Yeah, 30D should have been more cleverly clued. “Pig out, pig out” is ok, but maybe too obscure for Friday. I think “Avoid bacon, say” would have paired nicely with 31D.
I knew SAPIOSEXUAL immediately...I spent too much time on tumblr back in the day

Fun puzzle!
This is another topic: Why does the Times delete The Wordplay hints and info for older puzzles? I love to go back and do them but there is something unsatisfying about no key, no creator info. why do this? I support the games section and have subscribed to the Times for decades. 
Please put back WORDPLAY on all your old puzzles!

You know what else is a fitting  anagram of NOTES??? STENO, that's what!

Eventually finished this excellent puzzle anyhow - thanks for the fun!
solid funrelatively easy Friday 12m solve thanks Hemant!

That was kind of hard for me, at 42:38 unaided, but it wasn't terribly satisfying.

Some of the hard clues did yield an aha, but quite a few yielded a "really?" kind of feeling.

Not complaining.  Just providing feedback to the NYT team.

upon reflection post 12m solve no searches solved 'cleanly' what is the word for that anyway when you don't use google to verify an idea you had or spelling?  anyway fabulous Friday 5 stars the answers to the clues are what long time crosswords live for - its smart not obtuse - covers intellectual and societal themes plus has two Z's a rarity having Will come back to edit in my opinion has worked out smashingly well bravo - thanks Hemant hope Saturdays are this good!

One more quite unusual puzzle find. I stumbled on this one by doing a search for answers with the sequence of letters in our constructor's name. Anyway... A Sunday from May 24, 1981 by Louis Baron with the title: "Silly-Bus for Pun-Dits". I'll just mention some theme answers: HEMANTOLOGIST MATHLETICS GEEOGRAPHY FARMACOLOGY BINGUISTICS RESIDENTIST FILLOSOPHER HISSTOLOGY CAIROPRACTORS CHEERUPODISTS HEIRODYNAMICS Here's the link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=5/24/1981&h=33d ....
Elmo is not a monster!

Ok puzzle. Hard for me personally but nothing too foul. But really I commented to ask: what's with all the spam in these comments? I thought we had moderators but there are a ton of spam comments for that x word url.

Started with the NW corner as usual and just wrote everything in instantly.  I thought, "oh no, another Monday level puzzle on Friday".  But I was wrong, it toughened up and was a nice enjoyable puzzle in the end.  Well done!

“Breezy things enjoyed at the beach” annoyed me. 

Easy reads can be (and are) enjoyed at any time in any number of settings. Nothing inherently ties them to the beach.

Also, Elmo is entirely red, not just redheaded. I don’t know enough to say whether he was conceived as a monster.

Otherwise fun puzzle.
It's because of his feud with Rocco, isn't it?

SAPIOSEXUAL made me laugh! I thought I was attracted mainly to intelligence and humor but the smartest, funniest men in my life somehow turned out to be handsome too. I didn’t always recognize it right away. Sometimes I was the last to know.

This might have gone under the radar for a lot of people, but the answer for 56A was directly referenced in another Wordplay article for May 9th's puzzle, when referencing Ari Shapiro's similarly titled memoir. Seeing the clue here with the exact same title actually made me feel pretty good since I was rewarded for reading the Wordplay article, and I was satisfied seeing the symmetry in both radio journalists getting their proper recognition.

@Mark

Not to mention TERRY GROSS.
(Not to mention TERRY GROSS.)
@Steve L That helps explain my SE corner troubles 🙄
@Dan Thanks, Dan. This isn't the first time I've wished for an Edit button.

@Mark
Ari got his 15 minutes a few weeks ago.

@John M

Write to the email just above these comments.
@John M nytgames@nytimes.com
@Hugh what planet are you from? 😳 I loved it too. It took me 35 minutes. I’m quite happy.

userLocation: Doubt exists only in thought–not action.
commentBody:
@Mike
How formal is this class? Do I have to wear a cotan tie?

@Mike

That’s acute pun. I secant it.

Mike,

My daughter wasn’t allowed to take that course, because her student loan wouldn’t cover its fees. Something about the way she owes…

@Kay
I thought it was a California roll.

@Kay
Northern California - I grew up hearing “Hollywood stop”

@Kay

East coast, too.
Texas stop

commentBody:
@Bob
It's a reflection of the scarcity of vowels and the plenitude of consonants. In English, knowing a vowel eliminates 4 or 5 other choices. Knowing a consonant eliminates 20 other choices.  That's why XNGLXSH is recognisable and EXXXIXX is opaque.

Thanks for making me take another look at DAMS. I got it on crosses, and thought "meh, that's not great. Yeah, I guess a dam bars water from entering the gorge below. whatever."  But looking at it again I suddenly remembered dental DAMS, and it made a lot more sense.

Regrettably, the second act of the tale fails to live up to its promise, as shortly thereafter Welles apparently became very sleepy and passed out in a plate of spaghetti, forcing my dad and his friends to chat amongst themselves for the rest of the afternoon. At one point however, Welles apparently passed wind so loudly in his sleep that he woke himself up, calling out, “Steady! All steady now, gentlemen,” before retreating back into a deep slumber.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Nora

The Associated Press, NYTimes and Chicago style guides (and, indeed, most other American journo manuals) deem that "fundraising" and all its variants be hyphen-less.

Hence, "fund-raising target" ought to be "fundraising target."

That said, some authorities like M-W continue to recognize it.

So it's a nebulous nit.

(And a vaguely ironic one since it's the Times crossword.)

@Heidi

“Backyard” is pronounced with a stressed first syllable and an unstressed second syllable. “Back yard” is pronounced with both syllables stressed. Compare “The kids are playing backyard football” with “The kids are playing football in the back yard.” Read them out loud, not in your head, as naturally as you can.
OK then eye roll here. Unless that’s one word.

@Matt actually NYT style guide clings to fund-raising while AP and others have moved on to fundraising so whenever I read articles in the Times (or the crossword which adheres to the style guide) it catches me as odd. Like if they called it a cross-word.

userLocation: Doubt exists only in thought–not action.
commentBody:
@Elbridge Gerry
Nope. A left-turn arrow stops all traffic in all directions except for those turning left. Cross-traffic in both directions and opposing traffic are all stopped. Slightly better is two left-turn arrows for opposing directions. But all choices have problems. Trust me, I've stood on the corner of many a major intersection in the rain or snow watching all possible variations: flashing green allowing cars facing it unimpeded left, right, and straight travel; left green arrow that changes to regular green; two left green arrows facing each other; left green arrow facing right green arrow; etc., etc.

@Bob T.

But a U-turn is a kind of left turn, is it not? This solution does not eliminate left turns, at all...

@Julie Wait--as a former Michigan resident, I was going to give a different definition of Michigan left, although also specific to (just a few) boulevards.  It' s where a left turn is prohibited, so you go STRAIGHT, then you do a U-turn at a designated spot a bit past the intersection, then you turn right at the original intersection. I have never seen the version you are describing, though perhaps both exist. (And I think yours has to involve a U-turn after the right, right? Otherwise, right plus left equals continuing the same direction in which you began!)

@SBS until recently, I thought "Michigan left" referred to the fact that one can left turn on red onto a one-way street. But a few years ago they built some true Michigan left intersections around Ann Arbor and I learned otherwise. (And yes, it's a right turn from A onto B, then a U turn back onto B going the opposite direction, soon crossing over A at the point where you started but headed where you wanted to be.)

@NanuNanu

It’s actually when you take the puck and put a hard sugary coating on it. If the coating is too soft, then it’s just frosting.

userLocation: Doubt exists only in thought–not action.

commentBody: @NanuNanu In hockey a penalty is when a team has committed some infraction and they are forced to play shorthanded (ie. 4 players instead of 5) for a length of time determined by the seriousness of the infraction. While icing is technically an infraction, you would never hear it called a penalty because the consequence is not the same. When a team ices the puck (shoots it the length of the ice in order to get it out of their defensive zone) the result is that the play resumes back in the defensive zone and the defending team who iced the puck must keep the same personnel on the ice while the offensive team is allowed to change personnel, either for fresh legs or else to gain a match-up advantage. So "icing" is essentially the opposite of the "offside" infraction, and the reason it is not allowed is because it would result in a very boring game if it could be done without consequence.
